main_summary.summary: Juandalynn, Donzaleigh, and Ralph David Abernathy III, the three eldest children of Ralph David Abernathy Sr. and Juanita Abernathy, jointly recall their childhood inside the Civil Rights Movement. They describe early memories from Montgomery, Alabama: state authorities seizing the family car and auctioning family property to satisfy debts from the Montgomery bus boycott; the January 1957 bombing of their home; the Freedom Riders staying at their parsonage in 1961; and the daily presence of threatening telephone calls. They narrate the family's 1961 move to Atlanta after Juanita Abernathy insisted Ralph Sr. accept the call to West Hunter Street Baptist Church rather than work as SCLC staff. The siblings describe integrating Spring Street Elementary School in Atlanta alongside the King children, the Selma-to-Montgomery march in 1965, the James Meredith March, the Chicago campaign in 1966, and Ralph III's arrest at age nine on the Mule Train during the Poor People's Campaign in June 1968. They recount the partnership between their father and Martin Luther King Jr.—the division of labor in which Abernathy spoke on strategy and King on philosophy—and the assassination of King at the Lorraine Motel.
key_themes: Montgomery bus boycott aftermath and home bombing
Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. and the Poor People's Campaign
main_summary.historical_significance:
The interview is one of few first-person joint accounts from the children of Ralph David Abernathy Sr., the SCLC co-founder and post-1968 SCLC president. It documents the domestic and child's-eye dimension of the Abernathy-King partnership—the shared vacations, the swapped sermons, the engraved Movado watch inscribed 'To my dearest friend and closest associate'—that is rarely recorded in standard institutional histories of SCLC.

It also preserves specific, dateable episodes: the family's car seizure to settle boycott-related debts, the 1957 home bombing during Juanita Abernathy's pregnancy with Donzaleigh, Ralph III's 1968 Mule Train arrest in Douglasville, Georgia by order of Governor Lester Maddox, and Ralph Abernathy Sr.'s firsthand account of the Lorraine Motel shooting as relayed to his children. The participation of the Abernathy children on the front lines of marches—the Selma to Montgomery march, the James Meredith march, and the Chicago open-housing march in Cicero in 1966—constitutes documentary evidence of the deliberate inclusion of leaders' families in Movement demonstrations.

chapters.title: Earliest Memories in Montgomery
chapters.summary: Juandalynn recalls police officers seizing the family's new car when she was two or three, to satisfy debts incurred by the Montgomery bus boycott, and the auctioning of family property. Donzaleigh, born after the January 1957 bombing of the Abernathy home, describes the household's atmosphere of caution and the Freedom Riders staying at their parsonage. Both sisters dispute and reconcile their memory of Juandalynn cutting Donzaleigh's hair during a Freedom Rider gathering.

chapters.title: Fear and Nighttime Prayers
chapters.summary: Ralph III, born in 1959, describes his earliest memory as fear—of the house being bombed again and of nighttime in a room alone. He asks his mother to leave the hallway light on, and the siblings communicate by knocking on shared walls. They describe threatening phone calls during dinner, traveling the segregated back roads of Alabama and Georgia, and Juanita Abernathy's image of two angels over their beds.

chapters.title: Children Discuss the Movement
chapters.summary: The siblings reflect on whether they spoke among themselves about the danger. Donzaleigh recalls saying goodbye to her father each Monday morning expecting he might be killed. Juandalynn states she had to set the example for her younger siblings and that the family was anchored in religion and church community. They describe the King and Abernathy children as constant companions.

chapters.title: Move to Atlanta
chapters.summary: The siblings describe the 1961 move from Montgomery to Atlanta. Juanita Abernathy insisted her husband would not relocate without a church of his own, refusing the suggestion that he live as a paid SCLC staff member. The King family found West Hunter Street Baptist Church for him to pastor. Donzaleigh and Juandalynn describe meeting Yolanda King and the King children as their constant companions before and after the move.

chapters.title: Abernathy-King Partnership
chapters.summary: Ralph III analyzes the partnership of Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph David Abernathy Sr.: King delivered the philosophical 'why' of nonviolence, Abernathy delivered the strategic 'what, when, and how.' He cites his father's prior pastorates, his sociology degree from Atlanta University, his Army service to sergeant rank, and his student demonstrations at Alabama State University as the experience King depended on. Donzaleigh describes the engraved Movado watch King gave Abernathy inscribed 'To my dearest friend and closest associate.'

chapters.title: Integrating Spring Street Elementary
chapters.summary: Ralph III, Dexter King, Juandalynn, Donzaleigh, and Yolanda King integrated Spring Street Elementary School in Atlanta. Ralph III and Dexter played football and the team was undefeated; Donzaleigh and Juandalynn were taunted by classmates and graded harder by teachers. Donzaleigh recalls her third-grade teacher Ms. Elliott teaching her about racism, the PTA objecting to her papers placed at the top of the learning tree, and Juanita Abernathy enrolling them in afterschool tutoring at the Learning Foundation.

chapters.title: Children on the Front Lines
chapters.summary: The siblings describe Ralph Sr.'s insistence that his children participate in marches. Donzaleigh recalls the Selma-to-Montgomery march in 1965, walking with Jim Letherer, the celebrity fundraising mass meeting with Sammy Davis Jr. and Peter, Paul and Mary, and Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'How long? Not long!' speech at the Alabama State Capitol. She also recalls being tear-gassed in Cicero in 1966 and the James Meredith march in Mississippi.

chapters.title: Singing, Preaching, Acting
chapters.summary: Each sibling describes how the Movement shaped their adult vocations. Ralph III initially resisted preaching, served as a Georgia state senator and member of the Georgia House of Representatives, and later earned a doctorate in theology from the North Carolina College of Theology. Donzaleigh chose acting after meeting movie stars at the Selma mass meeting. Juandalynn became an opera singer, encouraged from childhood by Mahalia Jackson, who often stayed at the Abernathy home.

chapters.title: Ralph III Arrested on the Mule Train
chapters.summary: On June 15, 1968, traveling from Marks, Mississippi to Washington, D.C. with the Mule Train of the Poor People's Campaign, nine-year-old Ralph III was arrested in Douglasville, Georgia on the order of Governor Lester Maddox. Sixty-seven marchers, including roughly thirty children, were held overnight in the National Guard Armory. The next day they reunited with Ralph Sr. and Juanita at West Hunter Street Baptist Church. Donzaleigh recalls she was barred from joining the Mule Train because she was a girl.

chapters.title: Family Life with the Kings
chapters.summary: Donzaleigh recalls the Abernathys and Kings traveling together to the 1964 New York World's Fair and to California, sharing hotel suites with the boys' room, the girls' room, and the parents' rooms. She recalls watching the Sixteenth Street Baptist Church bombing news at West Hunter Street Baptist Church on September 15, 1963, freezing in the alley out of fear, and her brother taking her hand to lead her through.
related_events: Sixteenth Street Baptist Church bombing September 15 1963

chapters.title: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
chapters.summary: Donzaleigh describes the family learning of King's shooting by phone and television in Atlanta on April 4, 1968. Juanita Abernathy went to the Atlanta airport to meet Coretta Scott King, then went home with her to tell the King children before flying with her to Memphis. Ralph III recounts the account his father gave him of the Lorraine Motel: the shared room with two beds, his father's last conversation with King about cologne, the shots that followed, King dying in Ralph Sr.'s arms at the hospital. Ralph III also describes his father's succession to the presidency of SCLC.
keywords: assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
related_events: assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. April 4 1968

chapters.title: Carrying the Mantle
chapters.summary: Donzaleigh argues for her father's contribution after King's death: the Poor People's Campaign in Washington, the calming of urban unrest in 1968, and the words 'You may be able to kill the dreamer, but you cannot kill the dream.' She describes her father funding Andrew Young's congressional campaign through SCLC, paying for the King grave site at Daddy King's request, and using HUD Secretary Pierce's six-million-dollar grant for Abernathy Towers to benefit West Hunter Street Baptist Church rather than the Abernathy family. She closes with her father's last days and death.
related_events: death of Ralph David Abernathy Sr. 1990

loc_video.caption: Abernathy family oral history interview conducted by Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Atlanta, Georgia, and Stuttgart, Germany, 2013 October 10
